content.problemStatement:
Chefina has an array AAA consisting of NNN non-negative integers.

The beauty of a subsequence SSS is defined as 333 ⋅\cdot⋅
abs(means(S) - median(S))\texttt{abs(means(S) - median(S))}abs(means(S) - median(S)),

where mean(S)\texttt{mean(S)}mean(S) denotes the mean of subsequence SSS and median(S)\texttt{median(S)}median(S) denotes the median of subsequence SSS.

Now, Chefina asks Chef to choose a subsequence SSS of size 333 from array AAA such that the beauty of SSS is **minimised**. Your task is to find the **minimum** beauty possible.

As a reminder, a subsequence of an array is obtained by deleting some elements from the array without changing the order of the remaining elements.

Note: It can be proven that the answer is always an integer.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the number of elements in the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers, the elements of the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the **minimum** beauty possible of any subsequence of array AAA, having length 333.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 5·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N (N ≥ 3) – the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated non‑negative integers A_i (0 ≤ A_i ≤ 10^9).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum possible beauty of any subsequence of length 3. The answer is always an integer.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: After sorting [0,1,6,8] the best triple is (1,6,8) giving |1+8-2*6| = 3.\nTest case 2: Sorted array is [0,0,1,4,4,5,5,7,7,9]; the triple (1,4,7) forms an arithmetic progression, so the minimum beauty is 0.

solution.approach: The beauty of a triple (x, y, z) after sorting the three numbers (x ≤ y ≤ z) equals |x + z - 2·y|. The value is zero exactly when the three numbers form an arithmetic progression (y is the average of x and z). Therefore the problem reduces to finding three elements of the array that minimise |a[i] + a[k] - 2·a[j]| where i < j < k after sorting the whole array. After sorting the array we fix the middle element j and use two pointers i (starting at j‑1) and k (starting at j+1). While i ≥ 0 and k < N we compute cur = a[i] + a[k] - 2·a[j]. The absolute value of cur is a candidate answer. If cur == 0 we can stop immediately because the minimum possible beauty is 0. If cur < 0 we need a larger sum, so we move k to the right; otherwise we move i to the left. For each j the pointers move at most O(N) steps, giving an overall O(N^2) algorithm, which is fast enough for N ≤ 5000. The answer fits in 64‑bit signed integer.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2) per test case (worst‑case 25·10^6 operations for N = 5000).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the sorted array.
